Hezekiah was seriously ill, near death, during the year that the king of Assyria besieged Jerusalem. Isaiah brought a warning to Hezekiah to prepare for death. Prayer is one of the best preparations for death, because through it we receive strength and grace from God, to enable us to finish well. He wept bitterly; some believe this shows he was unwilling to die; it is in human nature to dread separation of soul and body. There was also something special about Hezekiah's case; he was now at the height of his usefulness. Let Hezekiah’s prayer, and understand his tears; there is nothing like having been under that fear of death, which brings bondage or torment. Hezekiah’s piety made his sickbed easier. “Lord, remember,” he says, not as if God needs to be reminded of anything by us, nor as if the reward could be demanded as due; it is only Christ's righteousness that purchases mercy and grace. Hezekiah does not pray, Lord, spare me, but Lord, remember me; whether I live or die, let me be yours. God always hears the prayers of those who are broken in heart, and will give health, length of days, and temporal deliverance, as much and as long as is truly good for them. Means should have been used for Hezekiah’s recovery; yet, considering how far the disease had progressed, and how suddenly it was stopped, the cure was miraculous. It is our duty, when sick, to use such means as are proper to help nature, otherwise we do not trust God, but tempt him. For the confirmation of his faith, the shadow of the sun was carried back, and the light was continued longer than usual, in a miraculous way. This wonder shows the power of God in heaven as well as on earth, the great notice he takes of prayer, and the great favor he bears to his chosen.

The king of Babylon was at this time independent of the king of Assyria, though shortly after subdued by him. Hezekiah showed his treasures and armor, and other proofs of his wealth and power. This was the effect of pride and showiness, and a departure from simple reliance on God. He also seems to have missed the opportunity to speak to the Chaldeans about Him who had worked the miracles that excited their attention, and to point out to them the absurdity and evil of idolatry. What is more common than to show our friends our houses and possessions? But if we do this with pride in our hearts, to gain applause from people, not giving praise to God, it becomes sin in us, as it did in Hezekiah. We may expect vexation from every object with which we are unduly pleased. Isaiah, who had often been Hezekiah’s comforter, is now his reprover. The blessed Spirit is both; ministers must be both, as there is occasion. Hezekiah acknowledged the justice of the sentence and God's goodness in granting a reprieve. Yet the outlook concerning his family and nation must have given him many painful feelings. Hezekiah was indeed humbled for the pride of his heart. And blessed are those who die in the Lord; for they rest from their labors, and their works do follow them.
